title: Amburana cearensis
taxonomy: Amburana cearensis is classified in the family Leguminosae, also known as Fabaceae, which is a large and diverse family of flowering plants. This family includes over 19,000 species, and Amburana cearensis is just one of the many species within it. The genus Amburana is a group of flowering plants that are native to the tropical regions of the Americas. The classification of Amburana cearensis reflects its evolutionary relationships with other plants, including its common ancestors and shared characteristics. By understanding its classification, researchers can gain insights into the biology and ecology of this plant species.
uses: Amburana cearensis is valued for its various medicinal properties. Traditionally, the plant's bark is used to treat fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions. In some parts of South America, it's also used to alleviate symptoms of respiratory issues such as bronchitis and asthma. Additionally, the plant's extracts have been studied for their potential anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. More research is needed to fully understand the plant's medicinal applications and potential benefits.

intro: Amburana cearensis, also known as Brazil copal tree, is a tropical plant native to the Americas, particularly in the Caatinga biome of Northeast Brazil, whose wood is used for making various products such as furniture, tools, and even musical instruments. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health conditions, including fever, respiratory issues, and digestive problems, due to its anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antifungal properties. The bioactive constituents of Amburana cearensis include flavonoids, terpenoids, and phenolic compounds, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Amburana cearensis include teas, infusions, and extracts, often used to relieve symptoms of colds, flu, and other respiratory infections.
